elements.text: Permissions
elements.html: Permissions#

elements.text: Permissions can be used to control what system resources the Node.js process has access to or what actions the process can take with those resources.
elements.html: Permissions can be used to control what system resources the Node.js process has access to or what actions the process can take with those resources.

elements.text: Process-based permissions control the Node.js process's access to resources. The resource can be entirely allowed or denied, or actions related to it can be controlled. For example, file system reads can be allowed while denying writes. This feature does not protect against malicious code. According to the Node.js Security Policy, Node.js trusts any code it is asked to run.
elements.html: • Process-based permissions control the Node.js process's access to resources. The resource can be entirely allowed or denied, or actions related to it can be controlled. For example, file system reads can be allowed while denying writes. This feature does not protect against malicious code. According to the Node.js Security Policy, Node.js trusts any code it is asked to run.

elements.text: The permission model implements a "seat belt" approach, which prevents trusted code from unintentionally changing files or using resources that access has not explicitly been granted to. It does not provide security guarantees in the presence of malicious code. Malicious code can bypass the permission model and execute arbitrary code without the restrictions imposed by the permission model.
elements.html: The permission model implements a "seat belt" approach, which prevents trusted code from unintentionally changing files or using resources that access has not explicitly been granted to. It does not provide security guarantees in the presence of malicious code. Malicious code can bypass the permission model and execute arbitrary code without the restrictions imposed by the permission model.

elements.text: If you find a potential security vulnerability, please refer to our Security Policy.
elements.html: If you find a potential security vulnerability, please refer to our Security Policy.

elements.text: Process-based permissions
elements.html: Process-based permissions#

elements.text: Permission Model
elements.html: Permission Model#

elements.text: VersionChanges v23.5.0, v22.13.0 This feature is no longer experimental. v20.0.0 Added in: v20.0.0
elements.html:
VersionChanges
v23.5.0, v22.13.0

This feature is no longer experimental.

v20.0.0

Added in: v20.0.0

elements.text: The Node.js Permission Model is a mechanism for restricting access to specific resources during execution. The API exists behind a flag --permission which when enabled, will restrict access to all available permissions.
elements.html: The Node.js Permission Model is a mechanism for restricting access to specific resources during execution. The API exists behind a flag --permission which when enabled, will restrict access to all available permissions.

elements.text: The available permissions are documented by the --permission flag.
elements.html: The available permissions are documented by the --permission flag.

elements.text: When starting Node.js with --permission, the ability to access the file system through the fs module, access the network, spawn processes, use node:worker_threads, use native addons, use WASI, and enable the runtime inspector will be restricted (the listener for SIGUSR1 won't be created).
elements.html: When starting Node.js with --permission, the ability to access the file system through the fs module, access the network, spawn processes, use node:worker_threads, use native addons, use WASI, and enable the runtime inspector will be restricted (the listener for SIGUSR1 won't be created).

elements.text:
$ node --permission index.js

Error: Access to this API has been restricted
    at node:internal/main/run_main_module:23:47 {
  code: 'ERR_ACCESS_DENIED',
  permission: 'FileSystemRead',
  resource: '/home/user/index.js'
}
elements.html:
$ node --permission index.js

Error: Access to this API has been restricted
    at node:internal/main/run_main_module:23:47 {
  code: 'ERR_ACCESS_DENIED',
  permission: 'FileSystemRead',
  resource: '/home/user/index.js'
}

elements.text: Allowing access to spawning a process and creating worker threads can be done using the --allow-child-process and --allow-worker respectively.
elements.html: Allowing access to spawning a process and creating worker threads can be done using the --allow-child-process and --allow-worker respectively.

elements.text: To allow network access, use --allow-net and for allowing native addons when using permission model, use the --allow-addons flag. For WASI, use the --allow-wasi flag.
elements.html: To allow network access, use --allow-net and for allowing native addons when using permission model, use the --allow-addons flag. For WASI, use the --allow-wasi flag.

elements.text: Runtime API
elements.html: Runtime API#

elements.text: When enabling the Permission Model through the --permission flag a new property permission is added to the process object. This property contains one function:
elements.html: When enabling the Permission Model through the --permission flag a new property permission is added to the process object. This property contains one function:

elements.text: permission.has(scope[, reference])
elements.html: permission.has(scope[, reference])#

elements.text: API call to check permissions at runtime (permission.has())
elements.html: API call to check permissions at runtime (permission.has())

elements.text:
process.permission.has('fs.write'); // true
process.permission.has('fs.write', '/home/rafaelgss/protected-folder'); // true

process.permission.has('fs.read'); // true
process.permission.has('fs.read', '/home/rafaelgss/protected-folder'); // false
elements.html:
process.permission.has('fs.write'); // true
process.permission.has('fs.write', '/home/rafaelgss/protected-folder'); // true

process.permission.has('fs.read'); // true
process.permission.has('fs.read', '/home/rafaelgss/protected-folder'); // false

elements.text: File System Permissions
elements.html: File System Permissions#

elements.text: The Permission Model, by default, restricts access to the file system through the node:fs module. It does not guarantee that users will not be able to access the file system through other means, such as through the node:sqlite module.
elements.html: The Permission Model, by default, restricts access to the file system through the node:fs module. It does not guarantee that users will not be able to access the file system through other means, such as through the node:sqlite module.

elements.text: To allow access to the file system, use the --allow-fs-read and --allow-fs-write flags:
elements.html: To allow access to the file system, use the --allow-fs-read and --allow-fs-write flags:

elements.text:
$ node --permission --allow-fs-read=* --allow-fs-write=* index.js
Hello world!
elements.html:
$ node --permission --allow-fs-read=* --allow-fs-write=* index.js
Hello world!

elements.text: By default the entrypoints of your application are included in the allowed file system read list. For example:
elements.html: By default the entrypoints of your application are included in the allowed file system read list. For example:

elements.text: $ node --permission index.js
elements.html: $ node --permission index.js

elements.text: index.js will be included in the allowed file system read list
elements.html: • index.js will be included in the allowed file system read list

elements.text: $ node -r /path/to/custom-require.js --permission index.js.
elements.html: $ node -r /path/to/custom-require.js --permission index.js.

elements.text: /path/to/custom-require.js will be included in the allowed file system read list. index.js will be included in the allowed file system read list.
elements.html:
• /path/to/custom-require.js will be included in the allowed file system read list.
  • index.js will be included in the allowed file system read list.

elements.text: The valid arguments for both flags are:
elements.html: The valid arguments for both flags are:

elements.text: * - To allow all FileSystemRead or FileSystemWrite operations, respectively. Relative paths to the current working directory. Absolute paths.
elements.html:
• * - To allow all FileSystemRead or FileSystemWrite operations, respectively.
  • Relative paths to the current working directory.
  • Absolute paths.

elements.text: Example:
elements.html: Example:

elements.text: --allow-fs-read=* - It will allow all FileSystemRead operations. --allow-fs-write=* - It will allow all FileSystemWrite operations. --allow-fs-write=/tmp/ - It will allow FileSystemWrite access to the /tmp/ folder. --allow-fs-read=/tmp/ --allow-fs-read=/home/.gitignore - It allows FileSystemRead access to the /tmp/ folder and the /home/.gitignore path.
elements.html:
• --allow-fs-read=* - It will allow all FileSystemRead operations.
  • --allow-fs-write=* - It will allow all FileSystemWrite operations.
  • --allow-fs-write=/tmp/ - It will allow FileSystemWrite access to the /tmp/ folder.
  • --allow-fs-read=/tmp/ --allow-fs-read=/home/.gitignore - It allows FileSystemRead access to the /tmp/ folder and the /home/.gitignore path.

elements.text: Wildcards are supported too:
elements.html: Wildcards are supported too:

elements.text: --allow-fs-read=/home/test* will allow read access to everything that matches the wildcard. e.g: /home/test/file1 or /home/test2
elements.html: • --allow-fs-read=/home/test* will allow read access to everything that matches the wildcard. e.g: /home/test/file1 or /home/test2

elements.text: After passing a wildcard character (*) all subsequent characters will be ignored. For example: /home/*.js will work similar to /home/*.
elements.html: After passing a wildcard character (*) all subsequent characters will be ignored. For example: /home/*.js will work similar to /home/*.

elements.text: When the permission model is initialized, it will automatically add a wildcard (*) if the specified directory exists. For example, if /home/test/files exists, it will be treated as /home/test/files/*. However, if the directory does not exist, the wildcard will not be added, and access will be limited to /home/test/files. If you want to allow access to a folder that does not exist yet, make sure to explicitly include the wildcard: /my-path/folder-do-not-exist/*.
elements.html: When the permission model is initialized, it will automatically add a wildcard (*) if the specified directory exists. For example, if /home/test/files exists, it will be treated as /home/test/files/*. However, if the directory does not exist, the wildcard will not be added, and access will be limited to /home/test/files. If you want to allow access to a folder that does not exist yet, make sure to explicitly include the wildcard: /my-path/folder-do-not-exist/*.

elements.text: Using the Permission Model with npx
elements.html: Using the Permission Model with npx#

elements.text: If you're using npx to execute a Node.js script, you can enable the Permission Model by passing the --node-options flag. For example:
elements.html: If you're using npx to execute a Node.js script, you can enable the Permission Model by passing the --node-options flag. For example:

elements.text: npx --node-options="--permission" package-name
elements.html: npx --node-options="--permission" package-name

elements.text: This sets the NODE_OPTIONS environment variable for all Node.js processes spawned by npx, without affecting the npx process itself.
elements.html: This sets the NODE_OPTIONS environment variable for all Node.js processes spawned by npx, without affecting the npx process itself.

elements.text: FileSystemRead Error with npx
elements.html: FileSystemRead Error with npx

elements.text: The above command will likely throw a FileSystemRead invalid access error because Node.js requires file system read access to locate and execute the package. To avoid this:
elements.html: The above command will likely throw a FileSystemRead invalid access error because Node.js requires file system read access to locate and execute the package. To avoid this:

elements.text: Using a Globally Installed Package Grant read access to the global node_modules directory by running: npx --node-options="--permission --allow-fs-read=$(npm prefix -g)" package-name Using the npx Cache If you are installing the package temporarily or relying on the npx cache, grant read access to the npm cache directory: npx --node-options="--permission --allow-fs-read=$(npm config get cache)" package-name
elements.html:
1. Using a Globally Installed Package Grant read access to the global node_modules directory by running:

    npx --node-options="--permission --allow-fs-read=$(npm prefix -g)" package-name

  2. Using the npx Cache If you are installing the package temporarily or relying on the npx cache, grant read access to the npm cache directory:

    npx --node-options="--permission --allow-fs-read=$(npm config get cache)" package-name

elements.text: Any arguments you would normally pass to node (e.g., --allow-* flags) can also be passed through the --node-options flag. This flexibility makes it easy to configure permissions as needed when using npx.
elements.html: Any arguments you would normally pass to node (e.g., --allow-* flags) can also be passed through the --node-options flag. This flexibility makes it easy to configure permissions as needed when using npx.

elements.text: Permission Model constraints
elements.html: Permission Model constraints#

elements.text: There are constraints you need to know before using this system:
elements.html: There are constraints you need to know before using this system:

elements.text: The model does not inherit to a worker thread. When using the Permission Model the following features will be restricted: Native modules Network Child process Worker Threads Inspector protocol File system access WASI The Permission Model is initialized after the Node.js environment is set up. However, certain flags such as --env-file or --openssl-config are designed to read files before environment initialization. As a result, such flags are not subject to the rules of the Permission Model. The same applies for V8 flags that can be set via runtime through v8.setFlagsFromString. OpenSSL engines cannot be requested at runtime when the Permission Model is enabled, affecting the built-in crypto, https, and tls modules. Run-Time Loadable Extensions cannot be loaded when the Permission Model is enabled, affecting the sqlite module. Using existing file descriptors via the node:fs module bypasses the Permission Model.
elements.html:
• The model does not inherit to a worker thread.
  • When using the Permission Model the following features will be restricted:
    • Native modules
    • Network
    • Child process
    • Worker Threads
    • Inspector protocol
    • File system access
    • WASI
  • The Permission Model is initialized after the Node.js environment is set up. However, certain flags such as --env-file or --openssl-config are designed to read files before environment initialization. As a result, such flags are not subject to the rules of the Permission Model. The same applies for V8 flags that can be set via runtime through v8.setFlagsFromString.
  • OpenSSL engines cannot be requested at runtime when the Permission Model is enabled, affecting the built-in crypto, https, and tls modules.
  • Run-Time Loadable Extensions cannot be loaded when the Permission Model is enabled, affecting the sqlite module.
  • Using existing file descriptors via the node:fs module bypasses the Permission Model.

elements.text: Limitations and Known Issues
elements.html: Limitations and Known Issues#

elements.text: Symbolic links will be followed even to locations outside of the set of paths that access has been granted to. Relative symbolic links may allow access to arbitrary files and directories. When starting applications with the permission model enabled, you must ensure that no paths to which access has been granted contain relative symbolic links.
elements.html: • Symbolic links will be followed even to locations outside of the set of paths that access has been granted to. Relative symbolic links may allow access to arbitrary files and directories. When starting applications with the permission model enabled, you must ensure that no paths to which access has been granted contain relative symbolic links.

sections.heading: Permissions
sections.html:
Permissions#

Permissions can be used to control what system resources the Node.js process has access to or what actions the process can take with those resources.

  • Process-based permissions control the Node.js process's access to resources. The resource can be entirely allowed or denied, or actions related to it can be controlled. For example, file system reads can be allowed while denying writes. This feature does not protect against malicious code. According to the Node.js Security Policy, Node.js trusts any code it is asked to run.

The permission model implements a "seat belt" approach, which prevents trusted code from unintentionally changing files or using resources that access has not explicitly been granted to. It does not provide security guarantees in the presence of malicious code. Malicious code can bypass the permission model and execute arbitrary code without the restrictions imposed by the permission model.

If you find a potential security vulnerability, please refer to our Security Policy.
sections.text:
Permissions
Permissions can be used to control what system resources the Node.js process has access to or what actions the process can take with those resources.
Process-based permissions control the Node.js process's access to resources. The resource can be entirely allowed or denied, or actions related to it can be controlled. For example, file system reads can be allowed while denying writes. This feature does not protect against malicious code. According to the Node.js Security Policy, Node.js trusts any code it is asked to run.
The permission model implements a "seat belt" approach, which prevents trusted code from unintentionally changing files or using resources that access has not explicitly been granted to. It does not provide security guarantees in the presence of malicious code. Malicious code can bypass the permission model and execute arbitrary code without the restrictions imposed by the permission model.
If you find a potential security vulnerability, please refer to our Security Policy.

sections.heading: Permission Model
sections.html:
Permission Model#

VersionChanges
v23.5.0, v22.13.0

This feature is no longer experimental.

v20.0.0

Added in: v20.0.0

The Node.js Permission Model is a mechanism for restricting access to specific resources during execution. The API exists behind a flag --permission which when enabled, will restrict access to all available permissions.

The available permissions are documented by the --permission flag.

When starting Node.js with --permission, the ability to access the file system through the fs module, access the network, spawn processes, use node:worker_threads, use native addons, use WASI, and enable the runtime inspector will be restricted (the listener for SIGUSR1 won't be created).

$ node --permission index.js

Error: Access to this API has been restricted
    at node:internal/main/run_main_module:23:47 {
  code: 'ERR_ACCESS_DENIED',
  permission: 'FileSystemRead',
  resource: '/home/user/index.js'
}

Allowing access to spawning a process and creating worker threads can be done using the --allow-child-process and --allow-worker respectively.

To allow network access, use --allow-net and for allowing native addons when using permission model, use the --allow-addons flag. For WASI, use the --allow-wasi flag.
sections.text:
Permission Model
VersionChanges v23.5.0, v22.13.0 This feature is no longer experimental. v20.0.0 Added in: v20.0.0
The Node.js Permission Model is a mechanism for restricting access to specific resources during execution. The API exists behind a flag --permission which when enabled, will restrict access to all available permissions.
The available permissions are documented by the --permission flag.
When starting Node.js with --permission, the ability to access the file system through the fs module, access the network, spawn processes, use node:worker_threads, use native addons, use WASI, and enable the runtime inspector will be restricted (the listener for SIGUSR1 won't be created).
$ node --permission index.js

Error: Access to this API has been restricted
    at node:internal/main/run_main_module:23:47 {
  code: 'ERR_ACCESS_DENIED',
  permission: 'FileSystemRead',
  resource: '/home/user/index.js'
}
Allowing access to spawning a process and creating worker threads can be done using the --allow-child-process and --allow-worker respectively.
To allow network access, use --allow-net and for allowing native addons when using permission model, use the --allow-addons flag. For WASI, use the --allow-wasi flag.

sections.heading: Runtime API
sections.html:
Runtime API#

When enabling the Permission Model through the --permission flag a new property permission is added to the process object. This property contains one function:
sections.text:
Runtime API
When enabling the Permission Model through the --permission flag a new property permission is added to the process object. This property contains one function:

sections.heading: permission.has(scope[, reference])
sections.html:
permission.has(scope[, reference])#

API call to check permissions at runtime (permission.has())

process.permission.has('fs.write'); // true
process.permission.has('fs.write', '/home/rafaelgss/protected-folder'); // true

process.permission.has('fs.read'); // true
process.permission.has('fs.read', '/home/rafaelgss/protected-folder'); // false
sections.text:
permission.has(scope[, reference])
API call to check permissions at runtime (permission.has())
process.permission.has('fs.write'); // true
process.permission.has('fs.write', '/home/rafaelgss/protected-folder'); // true

process.permission.has('fs.read'); // true
process.permission.has('fs.read', '/home/rafaelgss/protected-folder'); // false

sections.heading: File System Permissions
sections.html:
File System Permissions#

The Permission Model, by default, restricts access to the file system through the node:fs module. It does not guarantee that users will not be able to access the file system through other means, such as through the node:sqlite module.

To allow access to the file system, use the --allow-fs-read and --allow-fs-write flags:

$ node --permission --allow-fs-read=* --allow-fs-write=* index.js
Hello world!

By default the entrypoints of your application are included in the allowed file system read list. For example:

$ node --permission index.js
  • index.js will be included in the allowed file system read list
$ node -r /path/to/custom-require.js --permission index.js.
  • /path/to/custom-require.js will be included in the allowed file system read list.
  • index.js will be included in the allowed file system read list.

The valid arguments for both flags are:

  • * - To allow all FileSystemRead or FileSystemWrite operations, respectively.
  • Relative paths to the current working directory.
  • Absolute paths.

Example:

  • --allow-fs-read=* - It will allow all FileSystemRead operations.
  • --allow-fs-write=* - It will allow all FileSystemWrite operations.
  • --allow-fs-write=/tmp/ - It will allow FileSystemWrite access to the /tmp/ folder.
  • --allow-fs-read=/tmp/ --allow-fs-read=/home/.gitignore - It allows FileSystemRead access to the /tmp/ folder and the /home/.gitignore path.

Wildcards are supported too:

  • --allow-fs-read=/home/test* will allow read access to everything that matches the wildcard. e.g: /home/test/file1 or /home/test2

After passing a wildcard character (*) all subsequent characters will be ignored. For example: /home/*.js will work similar to /home/*.

When the permission model is initialized, it will automatically add a wildcard (*) if the specified directory exists. For example, if /home/test/files exists, it will be treated as /home/test/files/*. However, if the directory does not exist, the wildcard will not be added, and access will be limited to /home/test/files. If you want to allow access to a folder that does not exist yet, make sure to explicitly include the wildcard: /my-path/folder-do-not-exist/*.
sections.text:
File System Permissions
The Permission Model, by default, restricts access to the file system through the node:fs module. It does not guarantee that users will not be able to access the file system through other means, such as through the node:sqlite module.
To allow access to the file system, use the --allow-fs-read and --allow-fs-write flags:
$ node --permission --allow-fs-read=* --allow-fs-write=* index.js
Hello world!
By default the entrypoints of your application are included in the allowed file system read list. For example:
$ node --permission index.js
index.js will be included in the allowed file system read list
$ node -r /path/to/custom-require.js --permission index.js.
/path/to/custom-require.js will be included in the allowed file system read list. index.js will be included in the allowed file system read list.
The valid arguments for both flags are:
* - To allow all FileSystemRead or FileSystemWrite operations, respectively. Relative paths to the current working directory. Absolute paths.
Example:
--allow-fs-read=* - It will allow all FileSystemRead operations. --allow-fs-write=* - It will allow all FileSystemWrite operations. --allow-fs-write=/tmp/ - It will allow FileSystemWrite access to the /tmp/ folder. --allow-fs-read=/tmp/ --allow-fs-read=/home/.gitignore - It allows FileSystemRead access to the /tmp/ folder and the /home/.gitignore path.
Wildcards are supported too:
--allow-fs-read=/home/test* will allow read access to everything that matches the wildcard. e.g: /home/test/file1 or /home/test2
After passing a wildcard character (*) all subsequent characters will be ignored. For example: /home/*.js will work similar to /home/*.
When the permission model is initialized, it will automatically add a wildcard (*) if the specified directory exists. For example, if /home/test/files exists, it will be treated as /home/test/files/*. However, if the directory does not exist, the wildcard will not be added, and access will be limited to /home/test/files. If you want to allow access to a folder that does not exist yet, make sure to explicitly include the wildcard: /my-path/folder-do-not-exist/*.

sections.heading: Using the Permission Model with npx
sections.html:
Using the Permission Model with npx#

If you're using npx to execute a Node.js script, you can enable the Permission Model by passing the --node-options flag. For example:

npx --node-options="--permission" package-name

This sets the NODE_OPTIONS environment variable for all Node.js processes spawned by npx, without affecting the npx process itself.

FileSystemRead Error with npx

The above command will likely throw a FileSystemRead invalid access error because Node.js requires file system read access to locate and execute the package. To avoid this:

  1. Using a Globally Installed Package Grant read access to the global node_modules directory by running:

    npx --node-options="--permission --allow-fs-read=$(npm prefix -g)" package-name

  2. Using the npx Cache If you are installing the package temporarily or relying on the npx cache, grant read access to the npm cache directory:

    npx --node-options="--permission --allow-fs-read=$(npm config get cache)" package-name

Any arguments you would normally pass to node (e.g., --allow-* flags) can also be passed through the --node-options flag. This flexibility makes it easy to configure permissions as needed when using npx.
sections.text:
Using the Permission Model with npx
If you're using npx to execute a Node.js script, you can enable the Permission Model by passing the --node-options flag. For example:
npx --node-options="--permission" package-name
This sets the NODE_OPTIONS environment variable for all Node.js processes spawned by npx, without affecting the npx process itself.
FileSystemRead Error with npx
The above command will likely throw a FileSystemRead invalid access error because Node.js requires file system read access to locate and execute the package. To avoid this:
Using a Globally Installed Package Grant read access to the global node_modules directory by running: npx --node-options="--permission --allow-fs-read=$(npm prefix -g)" package-name Using the npx Cache If you are installing the package temporarily or relying on the npx cache, grant read access to the npm cache directory: npx --node-options="--permission --allow-fs-read=$(npm config get cache)" package-name
Any arguments you would normally pass to node (e.g., --allow-* flags) can also be passed through the --node-options flag. This flexibility makes it easy to configure permissions as needed when using npx.

sections.heading: Permission Model constraints
sections.html:
Permission Model constraints#

There are constraints you need to know before using this system:

  • The model does not inherit to a worker thread.
  • When using the Permission Model the following features will be restricted:
    • Native modules
    • Network
    • Child process
    • Worker Threads
    • Inspector protocol
    • File system access
    • WASI
  • The Permission Model is initialized after the Node.js environment is set up. However, certain flags such as --env-file or --openssl-config are designed to read files before environment initialization. As a result, such flags are not subject to the rules of the Permission Model. The same applies for V8 flags that can be set via runtime through v8.setFlagsFromString.
  • OpenSSL engines cannot be requested at runtime when the Permission Model is enabled, affecting the built-in crypto, https, and tls modules.
  • Run-Time Loadable Extensions cannot be loaded when the Permission Model is enabled, affecting the sqlite module.
  • Using existing file descriptors via the node:fs module bypasses the Permission Model.
sections.text:
Permission Model constraints
There are constraints you need to know before using this system:
The model does not inherit to a worker thread. When using the Permission Model the following features will be restricted: Native modules Network Child process Worker Threads Inspector protocol File system access WASI The Permission Model is initialized after the Node.js environment is set up. However, certain flags such as --env-file or --openssl-config are designed to read files before environment initialization. As a result, such flags are not subject to the rules of the Permission Model. The same applies for V8 flags that can be set via runtime through v8.setFlagsFromString. OpenSSL engines cannot be requested at runtime when the Permission Model is enabled, affecting the built-in crypto, https, and tls modules. Run-Time Loadable Extensions cannot be loaded when the Permission Model is enabled, affecting the sqlite module. Using existing file descriptors via the node:fs module bypasses the Permission Model.

sections.heading: Limitations and Known Issues
sections.html:
Limitations and Known Issues#
  • Symbolic links will be followed even to locations outside of the set of paths that access has been granted to. Relative symbolic links may allow access to arbitrary files and directories. When starting applications with the permission model enabled, you must ensure that no paths to which access has been granted contain relative symbolic links.
sections.text:
Limitations and Known Issues
Symbolic links will be followed even to locations outside of the set of paths that access has been granted to. Relative symbolic links may allow access to arbitrary files and directories. When starting applications with the permission model enabled, you must ensure that no paths to which access has been granted contain relative symbolic links.
